tasks.design: Deliberately zero-new-infra: a fixed-window counter in a module-level Map, same spirit as the existing src/lib/apiCache.js NodeCache pattern but a separate concern (rate limiting, not response caching). This is a per-serverless-instance limiter — on Vercel each instance has its own Map, so it does NOT coordinate across instances (same gotcha as apiCache.js's per-instance NodeCache, documented in root CLAUDE.md's Architecture section). A true cross-instance guarantee would need a shared store (e.g. Upstash Redis via the Vercel Marketplace, or a DynamoDB counter table) — root CLAUDE.md says 'no new infra ... unless explicitly asked', so this task deliberately ships the weaker zero-infra version as a pragmatic first cut: it still stops a single client hammering a single instance, which is the common case for a low-traffic personal site.

tasks.design: Hand-authored SVG (no image-generation tooling available to a builder agent) — a circle + a text glyph, deliberately simple rather than an exact font trace. Colour is `#FF5C39` (the `--accent-movies` CSS var's resolved value, `src/styles/globals.css:26` — hardcode the literal hex in the SVG since a static public/ asset has no access to CSS custom properties). Do NOT reference the Bricolage Grotesque webfont inside the SVG: it's loaded via a `<link>` in `_document.js`'s `<Head>` for the PAGE's own DOM, but a favicon is fetched and rendered by the browser chrome as a wholly separate document with no access to that stylesheet — an unavailable font-family silently falls back anyway, but naming a system default directly (`Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif`) is more honest about what will actually render. `font-weight:700` is preserved since that's independent of which font resolves. SVG favicons (`<link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml">`) are supported by all current evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari 9+, Edge); a `.ico`/`.png` fallback is explicitly OUT OF SCOPE for this task (no rasterization tooling available in a typical builder agent's environment) — note this as a known gap rather than attempting it.

tasks.title: Add list-aware gradient assignment helper to CoverTile/ReviewCard
tasks.design:
Resolved architecture decision: keep `gradientForKey`'s pure per-item hash as the PREFERRED/default colour (this preserves the existing 'same item always renders the same colour everywhere' invariant in the common case, since most items in a rendered list won't collide with their own recent history). Add a NEW pure function, `assignGradients(keys)`, exported alongside `gradientForKey`/`GRADIENT_PAIRS` from `src/components/CoverTile.js`. It takes an ORDERED array of keys (strings) — one per item that will actually render a gradient fallback, in the exact order those items will be rendered on the page — and returns a same-length, same-order array of gradient CSS strings (`linear-gradient(135deg, <from>, <to>)`, matching `gradientForKey`'s existing output format). Algorithm: walk the keys in order, maintaining a sliding window of the last (up to) 4 chosen `GRADIENT_PAIRS` indices. For each key: compute `preferred = hashString(key) % GRADIENT_PAIRS.length` (reuse the existing internal `hashString`). If `preferred` is not in the trailing window, use it (this is the common case — preserves the pure per-item hash colour). If it collides, deterministically probe `preferred+1, preferred+2, ...` (mod length), skipping any index already in the trailing window, and use the first non-colliding candidate — this is always guaranteed to succeed since `GRADIENT_PAIRS` has 9 entries and the window holds at most 4, so at least 5 candidates are always free; do not add error-handling for the 'no candidate' case beyond a defensive fallback to `preferred` (unreachable in practice, but keep the function total). Push the chosen index onto the window, trimming it to the most recent 4. This is a page-agnostic, framework-agnostic pure function — it knows nothing about React, items, or images; callers (task 2) are responsible for (a) filtering their item list down to just the keys of items that will actually show a gradient (i.e. excluding items with real artwork, where the variant supports real artwork at all) BEFORE calling it, in render order, and (b) zipping the returned array back onto their items by array index (not by key — key collisions/duplicates are the caller's problem to avoid, so keep the contract array-in/array-out, not a Map).

Second part of this task: give `CoverTile` and `ReviewCard` a way to receive a caller-computed colour instead of always computing their own via `gradientForKey`, WITHOUT breaking any existing caller that hasn't been updated yet (task 2 does all the call-site wiring; this task only adds the capability + keeps default behaviour identical). Add an optional `gradient` prop to the `CoverTile` component: when present (a non-empty string), use it directly as the tile's `background`; when absent/falsy, fall back to the current `gradientForKey(id || title || '')` computation exactly as today. Add an optional `gradient` prop to `ReviewCard` and thread it to all three variants that currently call `gradientForKey` themselves or render a gradient tile: `SpineCoverCard` (books) and the inline `poster-banner` branch (movies/tv) should use the passed `gradient` prop if provided, else fall back to `gradientForKey(item.id || getTitle())` exactly as today; `SquareCoverCard` (albums) should forward the `gradient` prop straight through as the `gradient` prop on its internal `<CoverTile ... />` call (which itself now supports the override per the CoverTile change above) — do not have `SquareCoverCard` call `gradientForKey` directly, keep relying on `CoverTile`'s own fallback when `gradient` is absent. Do not change `gradientForKey`'s existing signature, export, or behaviour — it remains the single source of the 'preferred' per-item colour and is still exported for any caller that only wants a single-item colour.

tasks.title: Wire every list-rendering page to use list-aware gradient assignment
tasks.design:
This task consumes the `assignGradients(keys)` helper + the `gradient` override props added to `CoverTile`/`ReviewCard` in the dependency task, and rewires every call site on the site that currently calls `gradientForKey` (directly or via `CoverTile`'s/`ReviewCard`'s own internal fallback) to instead compute a list-aware colour assignment ONCE per rendered list and thread the result in. General pattern at each call site: (1) determine, in render order, the ordered array of items that will actually show a gradient fallback (for variants/components that never support real artwork — `spine-cover` for books, `poster-banner` for movies/tv, which as of this codebase render a gradient unconditionally with no `imageUrl`-bearing path — this is every item on the current page/list; for variants that DO support real artwork — `CoverTile` directly, and `square-cover` for albums, both of which accept `imageUrl`/`thumbnail` — filter OUT items that have real art first, since those never render a gradient and shouldn't consume a slot in the no-repeat window); (2) map that filtered, ordered list to its keys (same key each site already uses for `id`/`gradientForKey`, e.g. `item.id || title`); (3) call `assignGradients(keys)` once; (4) zip the returned array back onto the original full item list by index (only the filtered subset gets a computed colour; items with real art get none/are unaffected); (5) pass the zipped-back colour into the `gradient` prop at render time instead of leaving the child component to call `gradientForKey` itself.

Seven call sites, by file:
- `src/pages/index.js`: TWO separate lists need this — (a) the 'collection wall' (`wallItems`, a mixed movies/tv/books/albums/vinyl sample rendered via `<CoverTile>`) — filter to items lacking `imageUrl`, assign in `wallItems`' render order, pass via `CoverTile`'s new `gradient` prop; (b) 'Latest takes' (`latestTakes`, a mixed movie/tv/book/album list currently calling `gradientForKey` inline for its thumbnail div) — filter to items lacking a real thumbnail/poster, assign in `latestTakes`' render order, use the assigned colour directly as the inline style instead of calling `gradientForKey`.
- `src/pages/listening/index.js`: the top-albums ranked list currently calls `gradientForKey` inline per row — filter to albums lacking `album.image`, assign in the rendered `albums` array order, use the assigned colour instead of calling `gradientForKey`.

Fix search placeholder truncation by giving .collection-search-input a real flex-basis
tasks.design: Root cause (confirmed by reading the full CSS chain): `.collection-search-input` (globals.css:2585-2593) is a flex item of `.collection-review-controls` (a `display:flex; flex-wrap:wrap` row, globals.css:427-432) but sets no `flex`/`min-width`/`width` of its own, so it falls back to the flex default `flex: 0 1 auto` — meaning its rendered width is its 'auto' flex-basis, i.e. its own content-based hypothetical size. That size is driven entirely by the bare `<input>` inside it (globals.css:2601-2608, `flex: 1`, no `size` attribute and no CSS `width` in `src/components/SearchInput.js`), which falls back to the browser's UA-default text-input width (the HTML spec default is equivalent to `size="20"`, roughly 150-190px depending on font/font-size — NOT related to placeholder text length; browsers do not auto-size an input to fit its placeholder string). Because `.collection-search-input` has `flex-grow: 0`, it never grows to claim extra space from its flex row regardless of what sibling sits next to it — so the difference between pages is NOT the sibling (SortButtons on review pages vs. the tag-filter Pill row on Projects) squeezing it; every page's search box renders at roughly this same small UA-driven width. It only becomes visibly broken on `/projects` because `placeholder="search by title or description..."` (33 chars) is the longest placeholder on the site — every other page's shorter placeholder happens to fit (or nearly fit) inside that same narrow default width, so the underlying under-sizing was never noticed elsewhere. Fix: give `.collection-search-input` an explicit `flex-basis` wide enough for the longest placeholder in the site's current font/size (Nunito, 0.9375rem, per globals.css:2606-2607), WITHOUT adding `flex-grow` — growth would stretch the search box to fill all remaining row width on every review page that currently looks correct (e.g. next to the much-narrower SortButtons widget on movies/tv/books/albums), which is a bigger, unnecessary visual change and risks a regression the idea explicitly asked to avoid ('spot-check the other review pages still look correct too'). Keep `flex-shrink: 1` (already the default) plus an explicit `min-width: 0` override (flex items otherwise clamp their shrink minimum to their content's min-content size, not 0) and `max-width: 100%` as a safety net, so on narrow/mobile viewports the box can still shrink or wrap (via the parent's existing `flex-wrap: wrap`) instead of overflowing its row.

tasks.design: Rate-limit pacing mechanism (owner asked to respect rate limits; the specific mechanism was left for conversion to decide): do NOT fire all of a page's searches in parallel and do NOT rely on a hardcoded 'safe' page size alone — a fixed 15-per-page assumption does not survive two consecutive pages navigated quickly (15+15=30 requests vs the shared 20-req/60s-window budget in src/lib/rateLimit.js). Instead, `backfillQueue.js` runs searches SEQUENTIALLY (concurrency 1) with a fixed minimum spacing between the START of consecutive requests, and on a 429 response reads `retryAfterSeconds` from the JSON body (every search route already returns `{ message }` with a `Retry-After` header + the same shape on 429 per src/lib/rateLimit.js's `checkRateLimit`) and pauses for at least that long before retrying the same item once, then continues the queue. This is robust regardless of chosen page size or how fast the owner clicks through pages, and composes correctly with the existing `withApiCache` wrapper on every search route (repeat searches for a title already looked up in a prior visit resolve instantly from cache without consuming rate-limit budget at all).

Read SquareCoverCard (src/components/ReviewCard.js:14-43) and its CSS (globals.css:326-393): `.square-cover-heading` (title + read-only StarRating, `justify-content: space-between`) is the first child of `.square-cover-body`, which sits inside `.square-cover-card` (`padding: 1rem`, flex row with a 100x100px `cover-tile-wrap` — see CoverTile.js:50, `aspectRatio="1/1"` passed explicitly by SquareCoverCard) — structurally identical in effect to spine-cover's heading placement (heading pinned at the card's top edge, inset only by the card's own padding). tasks.title: loop.sh: auto-deploy to production when the backlog empties out and site files changed since the last deploy
tasks.design: Owner-preferred mechanism ("even better" per the idea text) for guaranteeing the site actually ships when a run finishes, rather than depending on a deploy task having been authored at all. Coexists with (does NOT replace) the existing single-pending-deploy-task authoring convention in .harness/CLAUDE.md — this is a belt-and-suspenders fallback for the case where no deploy task got authored (e.g. a task was added via ralph-loop-add-to-backlog outside the /convert-ideas path, or the sibling consolidate-ideas.mjs enforcement task has a gap). Design decision made here, not left open: "did anything site-touching change since the last deploy" is answered by DERIVING the baseline from committed history (TASKS.json + git log), not by introducing a new state file — find the highest-numbered task whose title is exactly the canonical 'Deploy pending site changes to production' string and whose status is done, resolve its 'mark done' commit SHA via `git log --grep`, and diff src/ + public/ against that SHA. If no such task/commit is found (fresh history / pattern not found), fail OPEN (assume changed, run the deploy) rather than silently never deploying.

tasks.title: consolidate-ideas.mjs: auto-enforce the single-pending-deploy-task convention during /convert-ideas
tasks.design: Owner's option (1), built as the documented fallback alongside the preferred loop.sh-level option (2, sibling task harness-deploy-enforcement-1) — both are being built as belt-and-suspenders per the owner's own "even better" framing being additive, not a replacement. tasks.title: Build a shared Markdown/WYSIWYG-style toolbar editor component for review text inputs
tasks.design: NEW DEPENDENCY under consideration (owner explicitly asked for an out-of-box WYSIWYG-ish markdown editor - 'select text, make it bold, make it italic... if there are any Markdown editors out of the box... it would be cool' - this is a conditional ask, not a mandate, so evaluate fit before committing). PRIMARY CANDIDATE: `@uiw/react-md-editor` - a textarea-based (not a heavy CodeMirror/Monaco framework) React markdown editor with a customizable toolbar (bold/italic/list/link commands out of the box via its `commands` prop) and live preview; roughly in the same weight class as this repo's other dependencies. tasks.title: Truncate long book review text with a Read more/Show less expand toggle (fixes masonry column-height imbalance)
tasks.design: Owner's own resolution to the books masonry column-height-imbalance design fork raised by this cluster (see the companion idea bullet above): rather than any of accept-imbalance / height-aware packing / DOM-measurement rebalancing, the owner picked truncation — 'let's truncate reviews and let's have an expand button that people can click on if they want to read the rest of the review... if we pick maybe the average size of a review and truncate to that length, that would be a good way to start.' This directly fixes the masonry symptom because a bounded card height is what actually caps how unbalanced two round-robin columns can get — `distributeRoundRobin` itself (src/lib/masonryColumns.js) does NOT need to change. Truncation length grounded in real data, not guessed: a one-off read-only scan of the live `BookRatingsV4` table (53 rated books) during conversion found `review_text` lengths ranging 61-2145 characters, mean ≈259 chars, median 179 chars — the 2145-char outlier is 'Ultra-Processed People', the exact book named in the owner's original bug report, confirming the root-cause diagnosis. Use `maxLength = 260` (the mean, rounded) as the truncation constant. Scope this to the `SpineCoverCard` sub-component in `src/components/ReviewCard.js` ONLY — that's the variant `/reviews/books` (src/pages/reviews/books/index.js:178) and `/reviews/books/edit` (src/pages/reviews/books/edit.js:77) both render via `styleVariant="spine-cover"`, so both automatically get this fix from one shared-component change. Do NOT touch `SquareCoverCard` (albums — already implicitly height-bounded via its existing `splitHighlights(...).slice(0,4)` fragment cap, so it doesn't have this failure mode) or the poster-banner/default variants (movies/tv — no bug reported there; flag as a possible future follow-up idea rather than doing it now, since the owner's answer was specifically scoped to books). Implement the truncation math as a new pure, tested helper in `src/lib/reviewText.js` per this repo's 'pure logic in src/lib ships with tests' convention, rather than inlining string-slicing logic in the component.

tasks.title: Merge /programmes stats+charts into the top of /workouts (shared Push/Pull/Legs filter, own date-range), remove the separate page, and default the filter to 'all'
tasks.design:
IDEA-2 INVESTIGATION (resolved, no data/logic bug found): read src/lib/programmeStats.js in full and traced the arithmetic by hand. classifyProgramme() lowercases the title then does a plain substring match against 'push'/'pull'/'legs' in a fixed loop order — it is case-insensitive correctly (toLowerCase() is applied before .includes()), a title matching more than one keyword only ever counts once (first-match-wins, no double-counting), and a title matching none of the three correctly falls out of every per-programme bucket (and out of the 'all' bucket too, since aggregateProgramme's 'all' branch is `classifyProgramme(w) !== null`, i.e. 'all classified workouts', not literally every workout — this is a real, pre-existing semantic quirk, see caveat below). Cross-checking the four July sessions named in the idea (Pull 5 July, Push 3 July, Legs 4 July, Pull 2 July) against classifyProgramme's logic: exactly one classifies as 'push' (3 July) — which exactly matches the chart's reported 'Sessions: 1' for a Push-filtered view. The arithmetic is fully consistent; there is no classification bug and no data-source mismatch (both pages hit the identical GET /api/workouts?mode=all endpoint). The ACTUAL root cause is a small but real UX defect: src/pages/programmes/index.js:15 hardcodes `useState('push')` as the initial filter — every fresh page load defaults to the Push-only view rather than 'all' (which is first in FILTER_OPTIONS and is /workouts' own default at index.js:78 `useState('all')`), even though nothing in the UI strongly signals 'you are viewing a filtered subset' to a user glancing at a chart tooltip. That default (not a data bug, not a classifyProgramme bug) is what plausibly produced the owner's 'Sessions: 1 vs 4 visible' confusion. FIX: don't ship this as a separate bugfix task — fold it directly into this merge task by making the merged /workouts stats section default to 'all' (reusing /workouts' existing activeFilter default, see Do step 1), which structurally eliminates the confusing default as a side effect of the merge itself. Separately assessed and REJECTED as its own task: the duplicate month-bucketing observation (programmeStats.js's inline frequencyByMonth vs workoutQueries.js's exported bucketVolumeByMonth()). These are NOT true duplicates worth consolidating — bucketVolumeByMonth produces a zero-filled trailing-N-calendar-months window keyed off `workoutDate` for the server-side /api/workouts/stats route (a completely different consumer, unrelated to programmes/workouts UI), while programmeStats.js's frequencyByMonth produces a sparse (only-months-with-data) count over an already client-filtered (programme + date-range) subset keyed off `start_time || workoutDate`. Forcing a shared abstraction between 'zero-filled trailing window for one API route' and 'sparse count over an arbitrary pre-filtered client array' would just be indirection for indirection's sake, against this repo's 'keep it simple' convention — no action taken, no task authored for this.

IDEA-1 DESIGN DECISION (confident, not left as an open question): unify the Push/Pull/Legs control — the merged /workouts page keeps exactly ONE Push/Pull/Legs selector (the existing `activeFilter` PillGroup at workouts/index.js:78/154-159), which now drives BOTH the new stats/charts block above and the existing paginated session list below simultaneously. This is simpler, matches the owner's stated motivation (fewer clicks/less duplication), and avoids two visually-separate Push/Pull/Legs rows on one page. However, KEEP the stats block's own separate date-range control (3 months/1 year/all-time, reusing src/components/DateRangeFilter.js + src/lib/dateRange.js as programmes/index.js already does) scoped ONLY to the stats/charts block — the paginated session list has no natural 'date range' concept today (it's just paginated over everything) and dropping date-range trend analysis entirely would be a real feature loss for no simplicity gain; one extra control row is a reasonable, minor cost for keeping that capability. `/programmes` is deleted outright (no redirect) per the owner's own 'removed' framing and this being a low-traffic personal site — consistent with 'keep it simple, no new infra'. Note CLAUDE.md/README.md currently have NO file-map entry for src/pages/programmes/index.js at all (checked via grep — zero hits) — that's a pre-existing doc gap, not something this task regresses, but this task should still ADD a description of the new merged /workouts behavior to CLAUDE.md's file map row for src/pages/workouts/index.js.

Fallback note for the builder: visually verify ⎇ renders cleanly (not a tofu/missing-glyph box) in the deployed JetBrains Mono font stack via the dev server; if it doesn't, substitute a plain filled circle ● (U+25CF) instead — do not introduce an SVG or emoji icon either way.

tasks.title: Give archived /projects cards a flush corner tag instead of inline badge text
tasks.design: Owner explicitly wants active design exploration and floated 'earmark the corner and mark it grey' as one direction while saying they're open to suggestion. Weighed two genuinely different directions: (A) a corner tag/ribbon (the owner's own suggestion, refined into a flush rectangular flag rather than a rotated ribbon — rotation/clip-path corner-ribbon tricks are more prone to breaking inside the masonry layout's break-inside:avoid columns at odd card widths, so a plain flush corner rectangle is the lower-risk execution of the same idea), vs (B) a full-card treatment (desaturated border + dimmed content). Chose (A): it's the owner's own explicit starting point (so no need to interrupt them with a menu when one direction is clearly closer to what they asked for), it's a well-established, instantly-recognisable at-a-glance pattern (like a 'sold'/'sale' corner flag), it's lower-risk (touches only archived cards, doesn't alter the base card look non-archived users see 95% of the time), and unlike (B) it doesn't risk hurting the legibility of the description/tags text on an otherwise-normal archived project someone may still want to read closely. Placement: deliberately the TOP-LEFT corner, not top-right — this is the cluster's key coherence call, since the sibling task in this same cluster (commit-count promotion) is claiming the top-right corner for the star+commit row; putting the archived tag in the opposite corner means the two new visual treatments never compete for the same space. Once the corner tag exists, remove the old soft-text Badge from the meta row entirely — it's a straight replacement, not an addition, so an archived card doesn't show the same signal twice (once boldly in the corner, once faintly in the meta row).

tasks.design: EMPIRICALLY CONFIRMED before this task was authored (do not re-litigate — re-derive only if you have a reason to distrust it): jsdom does NOT reproduce the real-browser quirk this bug is about. Three throwaway jsdom experiments were run in this repo's actual jest environment (via `npx jest`, then deleted — nothing committed): (1) a long-content controlled textarea inside jsdom reports `scrollHeight === 0` and `clientHeight === 0` always (jsdom performs no real layout, so there is no 'overflow' for it to compute) — `scrollTop` is just a freely-settable numeric property with no clamping or side effects tied to it; (2) directly reassigning `textarea.value = ...` (the exact operation React performs on a controlled re-render) left a manually-set `scrollTop` completely untouched (250 before, 250 after) — the native browser reset-to-0-on-value-reassignment quirk this bug is about simply does not exist in jsdom; (3) running a full real toolbar click via `userEvent` against a scrolled textarea (scrollTop=300) left `scrollTop` at 300 immediately after the click, before the fix even exists. CONCLUSION: a naive test of the shape 'set scrollTop, click a toolbar button, assert scrollTop unchanged' would pass TRIVIALLY today, on the current buggy code, with NO fix applied at all — because jsdom never introduces the regression in the first place. Such a test would be a false-passing test and must NOT be the chosen strategy — it provides zero regression protection. INSTEAD, test the fix's own explicit save/restore mechanism directly, which IS observable in jsdom: `scrollTop` is implemented as an accessor (getter/setter pair) on `Element.prototype` in jsdom (confirmed via `Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor` walking the prototype chain from the textarea instance — found at `Element.prototype`, not as an own property on `HTMLTextAreaElement.prototype` or the instance), so `jest.spyOn(Element.prototype, 'scrollTop', 'set')` successfully intercepts every assignment to `el.scrollTop` — this was verified empirically too: with today's (unfixed) code, clicking a toolbar button on a scrolled textarea produces ZERO calls to that spied setter (confirming the current code never touches scrollTop at all, the actual bug). Use this spy as the test's core assertion mechanism (see '## Done when' for the exact shape required) so the test fails on today's code and passes once the fix lands — a real regression guard, not an environment-quirk-dependent one.

tasks.design:
T273 (commit d4d5f3b) correctly wired `{getThoughts()}` into `SpineCoverCard` (the books variant). 26 minutes later, T279 (commit cea04e9, 'truncate long book review text with Read more/Show less toggle') rewrote that same JSX block to fix a different, legitimate problem (masonry column-height imbalance from long book reviews) and in doing so DROPPED the `` wrapper entirely, replacing it with plain-text interpolation: `

{expanded ? fullText : previewText}

`. This is the current, still-live state of the code — verified both by reading src/components/ReviewCard.js (no `` in `SpineCoverCard`) and by looking at the existing screenshot scripts/visual-out/reviews-books.png, which already shows 'Piranesi' and 'The Left Hand of Darkness' rendering literal unrendered syntax ('A **staggering** piece of work. What lands: - the _craft_ - the pacing - the ending') while the equivalent scripts/visual-out/reviews-movies.png screenshot shows the SAME fixture text rendering correctly as bold/italic/bulleted list via the poster-banner variant. So this is not a fresh bug to diagnose — it's a known regression to restore, with the fix constrained by an additional requirement T279 introduced: truncation. Fix: re-wrap the SpineCoverCard body in `` while preserving the Read more/Show less truncate behavior — i.e. `{expanded ? fullText : previewText}` (both `fullText` and `previewText` are still raw markdown strings from `truncateReviewText`, which truncates at a word boundary and appends '…'; react-markdown should degrade gracefully on a mid-syntax cut like an unclosed '**' or a truncated list item — verify this doesn't crash or look worse than plain text via the visual check, but do not attempt markdown-aware truncation, that is out of scope). Also add `src/components/ReviewCard.test.js` (did not exist before) with at least one test that renders a book/spine-cover item whose review_text contains `**bold**` and asserts a `` element appears in both collapsed and expanded (post Read-more-click) states — this specific gap (no ReviewCard-level test, only the isolated Markdown.test.js) is why the T279 regression shipped green and was never caught. Finally, add `Markdown.js` to the `reviews-books` PAGES entry's `covers` array in scripts/_visual-harness.mjs (it currently lists Markdown.js under reviews-movies/reviews-tv but not reviews-books, even though the books fixture already carries real Markdown bodies via mkBook(..., true) for Piranesi and The Left Hand of Darkness) so a future auditor matching task scope to screenshots doesn't miss this page again.

tasks.design:
src/pages/reviews/albums/edit.js currently (lines 62-81) renders `{albums.map((album) => (
...
))}` inside a bare `

Re-verified directly against current code (2026-07-07): globals.css:1390-1404 today still has exactly the defect idea #7 diagnosed — `.review-edit-card` (1390-1393) has no border/radius/background of its own (just a flex column), `.review-edit-header` (1395-1404) has `margin-bottom: 0.75rem` and ONLY rounds its top corners (`border-radius: var(--radius-card) var(--radius-card) 0 0`). Read ReviewCard.js and Variant6Hybrid.js plus their variant CSS: the four card bodies actually rendered under the header — `.spine-cover-card` (books, globals.css:257-265), `.square-cover-card` (albums, :339-347), `.poster-banner-card` (movies/tv, :184-193), `.perfume-v6-card` (perfumes, :3549-3557) — EACH independently declares its OWN full `border` + `border-radius: var(--radius-card)` (poster-banner and square-cover also add their own `box-shadow`), confirming the idea's hypothesis: the card's visible border comes entirely from the inner variant component, not from `.review-edit-card`. Confirmed the JSX nesting in all 5 edit.js pages is uniform: `
...Edit link...
`. Important refinement beyond the idea's own text: simply deleting `margin-bottom` is NOT sufficient on its own. Because the header only rounds its TOP corners (bottom corners square) while the inner variant card below it rounds ALL FOUR of its own corners, removing just the margin would still produce a visible seam — a square-bottomed header sitting directly above a card whose top corners are independently rounded, plus two separate border lines meeting edge-to-edge (header's border-bottom immediately touching the card's own full top border) — which reads as a mismatched double-border/stepped-corner artifact, not a clean single box. That matches the screenshot's 'separate rounded corners' complaint, not just the gap. Concrete fix (CSS-only, no JS/component changes needed): make `.review-edit-card` itself the single unified box — give IT the `border`, `border-radius: var(--radius-card)`, a background, and `overflow: hidden` (so a rounded outer edge clips both the header strip and whatever the inner variant renders to follow one continuous rounded shape). On `.review-edit-header`: delete `margin-bottom: 0.75rem` entirely (flush against the content below) and drop its own `border-radius` (no longer needed — the parent's `overflow:hidden` + border-radius will clip the header's square top corners to match the outer shape); keep its `border-bottom` as the internal divider line between the header strip and the card body. Then add scoped descendant overrides so the nested variant card stops drawing its OWN competing border/radius/shadow (which would otherwise double up against the new outer border), e.g. a rule targeting `.review-edit-card .spine-cover-card, .review-edit-card .square-cover-card, .review-edit-card .poster-banner-card, .review-edit-card .perfume-v6-card` that resets `border: none; border-radius: 0; box-shadow: none;` — this is scoped strictly under `.review-edit-card` so it does NOT affect these same classes on the public (non-edit) review list pages, where they still need their own full border. This avoids touching ReviewCard.js/Variant6Hybrid.js at all — verified while reading them that neither needs a new prop; the override lives entirely in globals.css as a nesting-scoped selector. Also in scope: `scripts/_visual-harness.mjs`'s `PAGES` array currently has ZERO entries for any `/reviews//edit` route (confirmed via grep — only the public `/reviews/` index pages are screenshotted today). This is a second, previously-unnoticed gap: even a correct fix here could never have been visually confirmed by the harness because the harness never looked at these pages in the first place, which likely contributed to the original defect riding through unnoticed. Add 5 new `PAGES` entries (`reviews-movies-edit`, `reviews-tv-edit`, `reviews-books-edit`, `reviews-albums-edit`, `reviews-perfumes-edit`, paths `/reviews//edit`, each `waitFor: ['.review-edit-card']`, `covers` listing that page file + `globals.css`) so this fix (and any future change to this pattern) has an actual screenshot to check against, per this file's own documented convention ('a new page/API-shape → update PAGES/fixtures').
